title: Carved cherry jewellery box lid
subtitle: A chip-carved panel in cherry for a small wooden box lid
excerpt: Cherry is the traditional European carving wood for decorative panels: it has a fine, even grain that takes a chip cut cleanly, a warm pink colour that deepens to brown over the first year, and enough hardness to hold crisp detail without being difficult to cut. A small carved lid panel in cherry is a complete piece in a single session. This intermediate project covers transferring a simple design, making the chip cuts, and finishing with Danish oil.
sourceNotes: The Art Amateur vol. 47 (1902) for chip-carved panel designs and cherry as a carving wood. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for decorative lid carving in British furniture.

content.text: Cherry is a fine carving wood. The grain is even and the wood holds a sharp
chip cut
well. The freshly worked surface starts pink and deepens to a warm brown over the first year. A lid panel in
seasoned
cherry takes one session and shows the wood at its best.
What you need: wood

A flat, planed cherry panel sized to fit the box lid: typically 15 cm by 10 cm, 8 to 10 mm thick. The surface must be planed smooth; any plane tracks or roughness will prevent the knife from holding a consistent angle. Lime is easier to carve and more forgiving; pear gives a very fine surface comparable to cherry.
What you need: tools

A chip-carving knife with a short, rigid blade. A leather strop; cherry blunts a blade slightly faster than lime and the strop is needed every five to ten chips to keep the cut surfaces clean. Sandpaper at 180 and 240. Danish oil for the finish.
Safety
Hold the panel on a non-slip surface. The chip-carving knife is pushed away from the body; the free hand remains behind the cut at all times.
The work
Step 1: Transfer the design

Draw or print a design sized to the panel. Tape tracing paper over the design, trace the key lines with a sharp pencil, then tape the tracing paper face-down on the cherry panel and trace firmly to transfer. The graphite impression on the cherry is light but sufficient to guide the knife. A simple geometric design with clean straight lines is the best choice for a first carved lid; a six-point star or interlocking triangles within a border frame.
Step 2: Make all stop cuts
Press the knife straight down along each
stop-cut
line. In cherry, a firm, single stroke is better than repeated light passes; two light passes produce a ragged stop wall. Make all stop cuts in sequence before beginning the chip cuts.
Step 3: Cut the chips

Hold the knife at 45 degrees to the panel surface and push the blade along each triangle side, angling downward to meet the stop-cut apex. Two strokes per triangle, meeting at the apex, release each chip. Strop after every ten chips; in cherry, a single pass on the strop restores the edge enough to produce another ten clean releases.
Step 4: Finish and fit the lid

Sand the flat background areas with 180 then 240 grit, keeping the sandpaper flat and away from the cut edges. Apply Danish oil to the full surface: one coat, 15 minutes absorption, wipe off with a cloth. Danish oil brings out the cherry colour without obscuring the chip-cut contrast. Apply a second coat after 24 hours. Attach the carved panel to the box lid with wood glue applied only to the flat background areas, not to the carved areas.
Variations
A deeper relief is achieved by repeating the chip cut at a shallower angle on the same triangle; each pass goes deeper than the last. Two or three passes produce a pyramid rather than a shallow V.

Pear (Pyrus communis) gives the finest chip-cut surface of any European hardwood; the grain is almost invisible and the cut faces are mirror-smooth. Pear is expensive but worth using for a small lid panel.
Care
No maintenance needed. The cherry will continue to deepen in colour over the first few years. Re-oil with Danish oil once if the surface looks very dry.

attrs.intro: Problems in carved cherry lid panels.

items.fix: Strop the knife and re-cut the chip at a slightly shallower angle; the fresh cut will remove the rough surface from the previous pass.
items.cause: The knife was dull when the chip cut was made.
items.symptom: The chip surfaces look rough rather than polished

items.fix: Press the knife back into the stop cut slightly deeper, then repeat the chip cut. A stop cut that is too shallow leaves a thin skin of wood connecting the chip to the base.
items.cause: The stop cut was too shallow.
items.symptom: The chip does not release cleanly at the apex

attrs.heading: Common problems
